## [09:07 - 11:21]

Which is really how every movie starts, right? And certainly any movie that uses the hero's journey as it's, as its story arc, the hero has a problem and they are cast sort of out onto an adventure to try to solve that problem. And along the way they're going to run into mentors and helpers like Yoda and Obi-Wan Kenobi or like Mr. Miyagi or like Glenda or like, you know, the Wizard of Oz or the Tin Man or whatever it is, right? They're going to run into these people that help them move through and solve this problem. And that's, this is a shift that you really need to understand if you're going to use customer stories as the content that you drive traffic to in this traffic system, which I do recommend as long as you set things up using the hero's journey. So go and watch, you know, really great companies that create customer stories. Pay attention to the next, you know, set of movies you watch because chances are you're going to see a hero's journey unfold and remember that it's not when you set up your customer story for, you know, for your marketing to feature the customer as the hero and then bring your company in as the person that helped them move through that and solve that problem. So what have we talked about? We've talked about content that engages and subscribe, gets people to subscribe. So ungated and gated content. And we've talked about content that gets people to convert and ascend. And they really very different types of content. You know, the ungated and gated content is really designed to indoctrinate. It's designed to educate, move people past some barriers. But once you get around, you make that critical left turn in the customer value journey, it's time to start shifting the content that you're driving traffic to, to content that helps people make an informed buying decision. Things like demos and comparisons to your competitors and customer success stories.
